comments.title: Elemental colors
comments.content: Dear JMG, I listened to your podcast interview on Tarot, was inspired, and decided to give it a go. I got a deck I liked the look of and the corresponding 200 page instruction book. It is quite a read and the word "Easy" in the title was clearly an advertisers choice as the barriers to entry are quite high, however, I'm following the instructions carefully and will see what comes of it. I am doing the SOP every morning, (which has been transformational, thank you for that) and have noticed that the elemental colors of the deck are different than the SOP. In the deck water is gold (yellow), and wind is blue. The SOP is the opposite. This is throwing me a bit for an otherwise handsome deck. Could you shed some light on why the symbolic colors are different for these elements? Thank you for all your work over the years. 108

replies.title: Re: Elemental colors
replies.content:
Once upon a time an Englishman, a Frenchman, and a German got in an argument over a four-legged animal that was barking at them. The Englishman said, "I say, that's a dog." The Frenchman said, "Non, non, c'est un chien." The German rolled his eyes and said, "Nein, das ist ein Hund." Was it a dog, un chien, or ein Hund? All three, of course, depending on the language.

Color symbolism is the same way. It's a language, or more to the point, a series of languages, and the same color does not necessarily mean the same thing in different color symbolisms. Don't worry about it -- just learn each symbolism in its place, and remember that the map is not the territory and the symbol is not the thing it symbolizes.

comments.title: Witch Bottles
comments.content:
Hi Everyone,
A couple of weeks ago, there was some talk about witch bottles being potentially harmful but I was in the middle of lambing and very tired. All the ewes and lambs are doing well and I just have time to write this before going out to bottle feed Snowy who was rejected by her mother for having super sharp teeth.

Witch bottles are a very old technology and as such, have been extensively field tested. You simply cannot excavate a house in Britain without finding at least one witch bottle and they all contain traces of human urine.

Someone said that to think people were out to get you was kind of paranoid but she has either had very good luck all her life or has not lived in a village. Witch bottles are essential to catch the kind of low-grade spite that is hurled around in small communities all the time.

In America, the witch bottle has taken on a sinister meaning it never had in Britain. It is not meant to hurt or trap anyone. It is only meant to have the spiteful-magical energies seek out an undefended target and then earth harmlessly on the iron in the bottle. I think all that stuff about hurting people or spirits comes from the Hoodoo tradition.

As for where I got my information on witch bottles, I got it from a master of natural magic, our host.
Maxine

replies.title: Re: Witch Bottles
replies.content:
Maxine, could you (or alternatively, could the aforementioned master of natural magic) give some links or pointers to additional information about this tradition (in the good old British sense)? Especially how to do them this way, but any other background information would also be welcome.

Thanks,

Milkyway

replies.title: Re: Witch Bottles
replies.content:
Hi Milkyway,
I can tell you what I know and if I go too far wrong, I hope our host will help us out with better information.

First, the practice of using Witch Bottles is, at least, hundreds of years old. The idea is to put some of your urine into a bottle or jar, add some nails for the nasty magic to earth on, you can tie the nails together with a red ribbon to strengthen the enchantment. I would hope people would put in a small coin minted the year you put the bottle into the ground as an act of charity to future archeologists.

The urine has your energy signature on it. This left out in an undefended area (I hope everyone here is doing regular banishings in their house?) near the house. They used to be dug in under or near the doorstep.

The bad magic seeks out the soft target and earths harmlessly on the nails. There is no trapping of spirits or harm done to others. You can always pray to any deities you are fond of to strengthen the enchantment.
Maxine

comments.content:

replies.title: Re: Dreams...
replies.content: I don't know about dream interpretation, but one of the things always advised for lucid dreaming or just remembering dreams, is to write them down as soon as you wake up. This works very well but there's a warning before anyone starts. Going from a dead sleep to writing with the light on can make your eyes feel more sore than you might have ever felt before. It's important to know that ahead of time or it can cause a very deep reluctance to do it that's hard to get over.

replies.title: Re: Dreams...
replies.content: This is a situation in which text-to-speech is helpful. I dream vividly and regularly have 2-5 memorable dreams a night, so being able to just speak them in the morning without having to turn on lights was helpful when I was actively journaling.

replies.title: Re: Dreams...
replies.content:
There are lights that won't hurt your eyes. I use a hooga book light if I need to click on a light at night. The light is designed not to wake you up - it has an amber color - but it's enough to make your way to the bathroom without bumping into things. It has three settings (low, medium, high). I use it on low when I need to get up in the middle of the night or check the time. I can't stand having any other light when I'm trying to sleep.

Amazon has some big problems but this link will show you what I'm talking about. I've been using mine for over a year and really like it.

https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07Q87WJ7M?ref=nb_sb_ss_w_as-reorder-t1_k2_1_5&=&crid=VHLAK5TJOQ8E&sprefix=hooga&th=1

Chris in VT

replies.title: Re: Dreams...
replies.content: I've experienced and occasionally still experience a similar sadness upon waking, I linked it to leaving the dream - like each dream was a brief relationship, some of which were fleeting and frivolous and others meaningful to the point where I remember and reference them to this day. Over time and after discussion with others, I've determined that part of my work in this life appears to be done in my dreams, part of why it dreamtime feels so significant and so strongly affects me. I'm still not 100% sure whether that work is purely on myself or actually having effects external to me, though the later seems likely. I've found that getting to bed early enough that I wake naturally allows me to at least somewhat "finish" my dreaming which helps with the potential for sadness. Also, depending on your practice, it may help to pray to a deity, your ancestors, or even just state an affirmation before going to sleep.

replies.title: Re: Dreams...
replies.content: Robert Johnson's Inner Work is great basic Jungian guide to working with dreams and active imagination

replies.title: Re: Dreams...
replies.content:
Creative Dreaming by Patricia Garfield is geared mostly toward lucid dreaming, but it includes some helpfully concrete advice on how to go about remembering your dreams (including a technique for writing in the dark to avoid the problems mentioned above).

Dreams, a Portal to the Source by Edward C. Whitmont and Sylvia Brenton Perera is a Jungian guide to dream interpretation that I've found pretty helpful - it gives some practical advice on how to work out what symbols might mean to individuals, and it gives some insight into how the story/structure of the dream can be interpreted.

Hope these help!
Jeff

comments.title: Solar and Lunar eclipses
comments.content:
The Solar New Year as per the Hindu Calendar started on 14th of April, when Sun entered the first of 12 Rashis, Mesha (The equivalent of Aries). Usually, a typical year has 2 or 3 eclipses. This year, I noticed six - three solar and three lunar. All except one are not visible in India.

What struck me was that the eclipses all occur in pairs of 2 within a fortnight of each other. The first pair is on 20th April (Solar) & 5th May (Lunar), the second on 14th October (Solar) & 28th Oct (Lunar), the third on 25th March (Lunar) & 8th April (Solar).

A web search tells me that 6 eclipses per year happened last in 2011 and 2020 (Jan-Dec year though).

What do you make of this?

replies.title: Re: Solar and Lunar eclipses
replies.content: It's one of many celestial cycles. Since eclipses only occur when the Moon is close to the ecliptic, they tend to cluster that way, and sometimes you end up with three such clusters (each with one solar and one lunar eclipse) in a twelve-month period.  Such bursts of eclipses repeat semi-regularly across time, and will doubtless continue until the Moon disintegrates into a ring like Saturn's, a couple of billion years from now.

I find that committing to quiet observation is healing to the soul, mind, and body and can be magically informative. Once you have a magical vocabulary from any sort of practice/correspondence (like the elements in the Sphere of Protection), you will begin seeing links everywhere you look. One practice I love is to choose a small view (like a square foot of ground) and a large view (a place to stand and distant direction in which to look,) and then, two to three times a day, spend five-minutes observing each spot.

Also, practically, a solo camping trip is more dangerous than most people think. If you are not an avid outdoors-person, definitely get some instruction in basic first-aid and camping protocols. I'd heartily recommend making it a duo-expedition. Two people is safer, makes the work of building camp and cooking more enjoyable, and you can easily walk a few yards away from one another and still have a "solo" experience in nature.

May you be blessed on your journey. :)

1) What systems of magic do you feel competent to work with?
2) In what way do you want your retreat to transform you/your life? What options do your traditions in 1) give you to enact those changes?
3) Do you have experience with a system of divination and has that system told you whether whatever you came up with in 1) and 2) is a good idea?

I would also mention that it is possible to overdo the whole retreat business. Certain mindfulness retreats have gotten a bad reputation for taking individuals who weren't ready for intense spiritual exercise and throwing them into spiritual exercise in relative isolation for days or weeks at a time with traumatic results for the participants. Therefore I offer:

4) What would complement and balance the magic you are working per 1) 2) and 3)? For instance, mind-emptying meditation might be balanced by contemplation of scripture to fill the mind up again, and then the mental acts balanced with a period of hard physical labor or other exertion to bring you back in tune with your body.

And it's good to start small.

replies.title: Re: Solo Retreats
replies.content:
My first question would be: how comfortable are you being by yourself? Second question would be: how much backwoods camping experience do you have?

If you naturally gravitate to being comfortably alone, then slow and gentle integration of your magical and spiritual practice into solo outings will likely be a good experience for you. But if you are more of an extroverted social person who occasionally gets overloaded by the clamor (“the noise and bustle of life”), then a solo hike may not be your cup of tea.

Getting backwoods (wilderness) camping experience is not something you can acquire rapidly. Evaluate your experience and your gear and how well you do using it. I’d advise first doing overnights, then two-night outings. If you know someone who’s been doing outdoor multi-night hikes, see if you can go with them sometime. But the likelihood of knowing someone like that is fairly low, and of course they may not be willing to have you tag along!

If you are a natural comfortable introvert and are attracted to being in wilderness, then you likely are already doing something like what you’re proposing (unless you are VERY young.)

I’m of much more of a mystical than magical inclination myself, though I’ve been following along with the Levi book club (and exposing to myself and perhaps others my limitations in the magical realm.) So I have very little to offer on the magical component you mention. I will say from decades of personal experience that solo wilderness travel can expose you to all sorts of personal insights, physical discomforts, and joys.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) No. That's a product of the very tangled relation to matter you get in the traditions that gave rise to the Cabala, and it doesn't seem to be a problem outside that setting; it's basically the return of the repressed.

2) It's not something the CGD system teaches, though it's something that individual practitioners can choose to do. One thing that a lot of people miss is there are many things you can do in the upper reaches of occult and spiritual practice, and they're not all the same in terms of their results. It's not just that there are many paths up the mountain; there are many mountains, and the view from the top is not the same.

3) I considered it at one point. Since I've handed over leadership of the CGD to a group of capable initiates, it's their baby now, and if they decide to find an artist and commission a deck I'll doubtless buy a copy.

4) The most common misunderstanding of alchemy is the notion that it's all about a single process. Alchemy is bigger than that. It's not a single field of study, like chemistry; it's a general approach to knowledge, like science. There's an alchemical method, just as there's a scientific method, and if you apply the alchemical method to different subjects you get different alchemies. This has been true of alchemy since the most ancient times. So among the old alchemists, some practiced metallic alchemy, some practiced herbal alchemy, some practiced sexual alchemy, some practiced spiritual alchemy, and the list goes on.

replies.title: Re: Sphere of protection
replies.content: There's a thing called karmic culmination that shows up when you start serious spiritual practice. Knowing that you are not a Christian, I would like an answer about what you might have read. I'm here wondering if Jesus had previous lives as a human, or not.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Yes, it's a delicate question, but why not? ;-)

The occult traditions about Jesus of Nazareth I know best come from Dion Fortune on the one hand and several Rosicrucian writers on the other; all of them are Christian occultists, and they have a distinct Christology that is, ahem, not very orthodox. Dion Fortune put it most concisely: "Jesus is a person, the Christ is a principle." From their perspective Jesus of Nazareth was a human being into whom the Christ, an aspect of the Divine, descended at the time of his baptism by John the Baptist. (The human will of Jesus was perfectly aligned with the divine will of the Christ; if you're into the complexities of Christian theology, you know that this matters.) The Christ remained in Jesus until the Crucifixion when, as the gospels say, Jesus "gave up the ghost," i.e., released the Christ from his body, mind, and soul.

With this background I can answer your question. Yes, the occult teaching has it that Jesus of Nazareth had previous incarnations as a human being, during which he achieved the state of perfect alignment with the divine will that enabled him to become the vehicle for the Christ. The Christ, however, had not previously inhabited a human body.

replies.title: Re: Occult lore
replies.content:
1) The amateur radio society in your country ought to be able to direct you to up-to-date information; ARRL, the ham radio society here in the US, is my source for this.

2) Good heavens, yes. Many mages had very good collections, and passed them on to students or other mages.
